title-group.article-title: Steatotic Liver Disease at Primary Biliary Cholangitis Diagnosis: Association With Ursodeoxycholic Acid Response and Outcomes

sec.title: Background and Aim
sec.p: Steatotic liver disease (SLD) is becoming increasingly prevalent, leading to a higher frequency of primary biliary cholangitis (PBC) cases coexisting with SLD. However, data on its impact in patients with PBC remain scarce. The aim is to assess the impact of SLD on treatment response and prognosis in patients with PBC receiving ursodeoxycholic acid (UDCA).

sec.title: Methods
sec.p: A retrospective, multicenter cohort study including patients diagnosed with PBC enrolled in the Spanish ColHai registry.

sec.title: Results
sec.p: A total of 469 patients with available data required for the study were included. Among them, 158 (33.7%) had SLD, and 124 (78.5%) met the diagnostic criteria for MASLD. Patients with PBC and SLD had lower baseline levels of alkaline phosphatase and higher liver stiffness at diagnosis. Regarding treatment response, no significant differences were observed across the different response criteria (Paris II, GLOBE, UK‐PBC, deep response and complete normalization). Additionally, the presence of SLD was not associated with UDCA response at 1‐year (Paris II: OR 1.04 [0.69–1.56]; GLOBE: OR 0.91 [0.58–1.44]; deep response: OR 1.08 [0.72–1.63]; complete normalization: OR 0.82 [0.52–1.30]). Baseline SLD was not associated with an increased risk of liver related‐events in either the univariate analysis (HR 1.19, 95% CI 0.71–2.02) or in the different multivariate models evaluated. After inverse probability of treatment weighting (IPTW) using propensity scores, SLD still showed no significant impact on hepatic event development (HR 1.62; 95% CI 0.92–2.83).

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: Although SLD is frequently observed in PBC, it does not appear to adversely affect UDCA response or long‐term hepatic outcomes.
Keywords: cholestasis, MASLD, primary biliary cholangitis, prognosis

abstract.title: Executive Summary
abstract.p: The coexistence of Steatotic Liver Disease and Primary Biliary Cholangitis does not appear to confer a worse prognosis to these patients. The therapeutic goal should focus on achieving treatment response within the first year of treatment initiation.

sec.p: Revised 2026 Apr 20; Received 2025 Nov 9; Accepted 2026 May 15; Issue date 2026 Jul.

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: It is well known that the simultaneous presence of two liver diseases implies a greater challenge in patient management and is usually associated with a worse prognosis [1]. Primary biliary cholangitis (PBC) is a chronic cholestatic liver disease classified as a rare liver disease. In contrast to the rarity of PBC is metabolic dysfunction‐associated steatotic liver disease (MASLD), the most prevalent chronic liver disease, affecting approximately 25%–30% of the general population and up to 20% of young individuals [2]. Its prevalence and incidence are higher among patients with metabolic risk factors such as hypercholesterolemia, diabetes mellitus and/or obesity [3]. In this regard, it is also noteworthy that PBC is frequently associated with dyslipidemia, predominantly characterized by elevated low‐density lipoproteins (LDL) cholesterol levels, a well‐established risk factor for the development of MASLD.

Given the high prevalence of MASLD, it is increasingly common, in daily clinical practice, to find patients with PBC in whom steatosis is identified, either through imaging studies performed during the diagnosis or in the follow‐up. However, data regarding the impact of Steatotic Liver Disease (SLD) in patients diagnosed with PBC remain limited, with only a few reports available in the literature, and their findings are occasionally contradictory [4, 5]. Thus, the aim of our study was to assess the impact of SLD and metabolic comorbidities on the response, prognosis and progression of patients with PBC treated with ursodeoxycholic acid (UDCA) in a large Spanish cohort.

sec.title: Patients
sec.p: We performed a retrospective, multicenter cohort study of patients diagnosed with PBC in 35 centres in Spain participating in the ColHai (the Spanish Registry for Cholestatic and Autoimmune Liver Diseases) registry. Participation in the registry was authorized by the Institutional Review Board and Ethics Committee at all participating centres in accordance with local regulations. All patients provided informed consent prior to enrollment in the registry, which was reviewed and approved by the Ethics Committee at each centre.

The inclusion criteria were as follows: (1) Diagnosis of PBC according to European [6] and Spanish [7] guidelines, (2) 18 years of age or older at diagnosis, and (3) standard treatment with UDCA. The exclusion criteria were as follows: (1) the presence of variant forms with other autoimmune diseases, (2) lack of response data at 1‐year follow‐up and (3) absence of information on metabolic risk factors (Flowchart available in Figure S1).

sec.title: Data Collected for the Analysis
sec.p: For this study, the inclusion date were defined as the initiation of UDCA therapy, which served as the baseline. We recorded demographic variables and the presence of other conditions, including autoimmune and metabolic disorders, as well as serological, laboratory, and treatment‐related parameters, both at baseline and throughout follow‐up. Liver stiffness measurements (LSM) obtained using transient elastography (TE) were collected at the time of diagnosis (before the initiation of UDCA) and during follow‐up, when available. Only valid measurements, defined as those with an interquartile range (IQR)‐to‐median ratio < 30%, were considered for analysis. The presence of SLD was assessed in all patients at the time of PBC diagnosis, prior to the initiation of UDCA treatment.

sec.title: Definitions
sec.p: Classic PBC was defined as the diagnosis of PBC according to guidelines [6, 7], in the absence of SLD. SLD was defined as the presence of compatible findings on baseline ultrasound imaging or a Controlled Attenuation Parameter (CAP) value > 275 dB/m [8] obtained at the time of diagnosis. In patients who underwent liver biopsy for the diagnosis of PBC, steatosis was considered present when it involved more than 5% of the hepatic parenchyma in the specimen [9]. Although the definition of hypercholesterolemia may vary depending on specific clinical contexts [10], an LDL threshold of > 130 mg/dL [11] or the use of lipid‐lowering treatment is usually considered. Hypertriglyceridemia was defined as triglyceride levels > 150 mg/dL or if the patient was under treatment for this condition [11]. Metabolic syndrome was defined in patients who met at least three of the following criteria: (a) increased waist circumference (> 94 cm in men, > 80 cm in women); (b) triglyceride levels > 150 mg/dL; (c) High‐density lipoproteins (HDL) cholesterol < 40 mg/dL in men, < 50 mg/dL in women; (d) arterial hypertension (HTN), defined as systolic blood pressure > 130 mmHg and diastolic blood pressure > 85 mmHg or if the patient was under treatment; (e) fasting plasma glucose > 100 mg/dL [12]. Overweight was defined as a body mass index (BMI) greater than 25, and obesity as a BMI ≥ 30. MASLD was defined as the presence of hepatic steatosis, diagnosed by imaging or biopsy [13, 14], in conjunction with one cardiometabolic risk factor and no other discernible cause as stated in the latest consensus [14]. Thus, those with no metabolic parameters and no known cause were deemed to have cryptogenic SLD (cSLD) [14]. Harmful alcohol consumption was defined as consumption of ≥ 2 units/day in women and ≥ 3 units/day in men [15]. Cirrhosis was diagnosed according to the presence of the typical clinical, ultrasound and/or histological characteristics and/or LSM > 16.9 kPa. Different response criteria were used to evaluate response after 1 year of UDCA: (1) the Paris II criteria [16]; (2) alkaline phosphatase (ALP) normalization; (3) ALP normalization + bilirubin < 0.6 × ULN (deep response); (4) Complete response, defined as normalization of ALP, gamma‐glutamyl transferase (GGT), aspartate aminotransferase (AST), alanine aminotransferase (ALT), and total bilirubin levels. Prognosis was assessed using the GLOBE score, with a cutoff value greater than 0.3 indicating poor prognosis [17] and the UK‐PBC score [18].

sec.title: Endpoints
sec.p: To evaluate (a) the response to UDCA at 1 year after treatment initiation, (b) the assessment of the performance of the different response criteria and prognostic scores in patients with PBC and SLD, and (c) the development of liver‐related events. A liver‐related event was defined as any of the following: progression to liver cirrhosis in patients without prior advanced fibrosis, hepatic decompensation in patients with previously compensated cirrhosis, development of hepatocellular carcinoma, liver‐related death or liver transplantation. For analysis purposes, only the first occurring event and its corresponding date were considered.

sec.title: Statistical Analyses
sec.p: Fisher's exact test was used to compare categorical variables, and non‐parametric methods were used for quantitative variables: Mann–Whitney (two groups) or Kruskal–Wallis (more than two groups) tests for independent data, and the Wilcoxon signed‐rank test (two groups) or Friedman test (more than two groups) for dependent data. Logistic regression models were used to estimate the odds ratio (OR) and confidence intervals (CI). The impact of SLD on the risk of liver‐related events was assessed using Cox proportional hazards regression and survival curves were estimated using the Kaplan–Meier method. Since the primary objective was to evaluate this impact in the context of UDCA therapy, patients who initiated second‐line treatment were censored at the time of treatment escalation (on‐treatment analysis). Time to event was calculated from the date of PBC diagnosis to the date of the first liver‐related event, initiation of second‐line therapy, death, or last available follow‐up visit, whichever occurred first. The proportional hazards assumption was verified using the Schoenfeld residuals test. As a sensitivity analysis, initiation of second‐line therapy was treated as a competing risk using the Fine‐Grey regression model, with results expressed as subdistribution hazard ratios (sHR). The level of significance was set at 5% (two‐sided). All analyses were performed using Stata Statistical Software (Release 14.1; StataCorp LLC, College Station, TX).

sec.title: Results
sec.p: A total of 469 patients were included, all of whom had detailed baseline and one‐year follow‐up data, as well as documented information regarding the development of liver‐related events during follow‐up, the presence of metabolic comorbidities, and the existence or absence of hepatic steatosis. Baseline characteristics of the cohort are summarized in Table 1. The diagnosis of SLD was established by ultrasound in most patients (n = 368 patients, 78.5%). Additionally, 65 patients (13.9%) had information on steatosis based on both ultrasound and CAP; 19 patients (4%) via ultrasound and histological assessment; 11 patients (2.4%) through CAP alone; four patients (0.8%) had data from ultrasound, CAP, and liver biopsy; and two patients (0.4%) were diagnosed based solely on histology.

caption.p: Baseline characteristics of the cohort.

fn.p: Note: Bold value indicates statistically significant values with a p‐value below 0.05.

Abbreviations: ALP, alkaline phosphatase; ALT, alanine aminotransferase; AMA, Antimitochondrial antibodies; APRI, AST to Platelet Ratio Index; AST, aspartate aminotransferase; BMI, body mass index; FIB‐4, fibrosis‐4 Index, GGT, gamma‐glutamyl transferase; IQR, interquartile range; MASLD, metabolic dysfunction–associated steatotic liver disease, OH, alcohol consumption.

sec.p: In brief, most patients were women, with a median age of 55 (IQR 47–63) years old. Most patients (87.6%) tested positive for antimitochondrial antibodies (AMA). Only 33 individuals (7%) were reported to have cirrhosis at the time of PBC diagnosis.

More than half of the patients (58%) were overweight or obese, one‐third (35%) had dyslipidaemia, just over a quarter (27.7%) had HTN, and 11.3% had type 2 diabetes mellitus (T2DM). Additionally, 124 patients (28.5%) met the diagnostic criteria for MASLD. Regarding baseline LSM, 34 patients (20.2%) of those with available TE at the time of diagnosis had LSM values exceeding 8 kPa.

sec.title: Baseline Characteristics According to the Presence of Steatosis
sec.p: Metabolic comorbidities were significantly more common among patients with liver steatosis compared to those without (Table 1). Among patients with steatosis, 97 (74%) were overweight or obese, 58 (36.7%) had HTN, 27 (17.1%) had T2DM and 67 (42%) had dyslipidaemia. In contrast, among patients without steatosis, 34 (14%) were obese, 72 (23.1%) had HTN, 26 (8.4%) had T2DM and 97 (31.2%) had dyslipidaemia. Notably, most patients with steatosis (n = 124, 78.5%) also fulfilled diagnostic criteria for MASLD.

Patients with PBC and SLD at the time of diagnosis had significantly lower baseline levels of ALP compared to those without steatosis (p < 0.001). Although no significant differences were observed in other biochemical parameters, patients with SLD had significantly higher baseline LSM (6.7 vs. 6 kPa, p = 0.008) and more frequently had values above 8 kPa (28.3% vs. 15.6%, p = 0.042). No significant differences were found in non‐invasive fibrosis scores between groups (FIB‐4: 1.21 vs. 1.31, p = 0.757; APRI: 0.44 vs. 0.45, p = 0.418).

sec.title: Patients With MASLD Did Not Exhibit Distinct Baseline Characteristics Compared to the Overall Steatosis Group
sec.p: When baseline characteristics were analysed specifically according to the presence or absence of MASLD‐excluding patients with cSLD‐, no major differences were identified beyond those already described for the group of patients with SLD. As with the whole SLD group, patients with MASLD showed significantly lower baseline ALP levels compared to those without SLD, as well as higher elastography values (Table S1).

sec.title: The Presence of SLD Did Not Impact UDCA Response
sec.p: Regarding treatment response, 320 patients (68.2%) met the Paris II response criteria at 1 year after treatment initiation, and 360 patients (76.8%) had a favourable GLOBE score (< 0.3) at the same timepoint. Approximately half of the cohort (232 patients, 49.5%) achieved ALP normalization at 1 year, 149 (31.8%) normalized ALP and had a total bilirubin level ≤ 0.6 × ULN, and 110 patients (28.1%) achieved complete biochemical normalization. Patients with classic PBC showed a greater percentage reduction in ALP compared to those with steatosis (39.6% vs. 29.8%, p = 0.005).

At 1 year after starting UDCA, we found no significant differences in any of the response or prognostic criteria based on the presence of MASLD or cSLD [14] compared to patients with classic PBC (Figure 1 and Table S2). Moreover, neither the presence of steatosis nor MASLD was associated with the likelihood of achieving response (Figure 2): Paris II (OR 1.04; 95% CI 0.69–1.56), GLOBE score < 0.3 (OR 0.91; 95% CI 0.58–1.44), ALP normalization (OR 1.07; 95% CI 0.73–1.57), ALP normalization plus total bilirubin ≤ 0.6 × ULN (OR 1.08; 95% CI 0.72–1.63), or complete biochemical normalization (OR 0.82; 95% CI 0.52–1.30). We also found no differences in the 5‐year (p = 0.9), 10‐year (p = 0.673), and 15‐year (p = 0.657) risk measured by the UK‐PBC score.

caption.p: Proportion of patients achieving each biochemical response criterion at 1 year of UDCA therapy, stratified by subtype: Classic PBC (no steatosis), cryptogenic SLD, and MASLD. p‐values correspond to comparisons across the three groups. ALP, alkaline phosphatase; Bil, total bilirubin; MASLD, metabolic dysfunction‐associated steatotic liver disease; SLD, steatotic liver disease.

Association between Steatotic Liver Disease and the probability of achieving biochemical response to ursodeoxycholic acid. Odds ratios with 95% confidence intervals are shown for each response criterion. ALP, alkaline phosphatase.

sec.title: Patients With MASLD Exhibited Higher Liver Stiffness Values 1 Year After Treatment Initiation
sec.p: The median LSM of the cohort at 1 year was 6 kPa. A reduction in liver stiffness values compared to baseline was observed across all subgroups (patients with MASLD, cSLD and those with classic PBC). The decrease in liver stiffness was more pronounced in patients with MASLD (from 6.7 kPa at baseline to 6.3 kPa at 1 year), although this difference did not reach statistical significance (p = 0.074).

However, consistent with baseline findings, patients with MASLD continued to exhibit significantly higher liver stiffness values at 1 year compared to those without steatosis (p = 0.02). Additionally, the proportion of patients with TE values greater than 8 kPa at 1 year was significantly higher in the MASLD group (p = 0.026), but no differences were found when considering 10 kPa as cutoff (p = 0.193). No significant differences between groups were observed in non‐invasive fibrosis scores, including FIB‐4 and APRI (Table 2).

caption.p: Non‐invasive markers of liver disease severity at 1 year of UDCA therapy, according to SLD status.

fn.p: Note: Bold value indicates statistically significant values with a p‐value below 0.05.

Abbreviations: APRI, AST to Platelet Ratio Index; FIB‐4, Fibrosis‐4 Index; IQR, interquartile range; LSM, liver stiffness measurements; MASLD, metabolic dysfunction‐associated steatotic liver disease; PBC, primary biliary cholangitis; SLD, steatotic liver disease.
Transient elastography available in 129 patients at 1 year after ursodeoxycholic acid initiation.

sec.title: The Presence of SLD at Baseline Was Not Associated With an Increased Risk of Liver‐Related Events
sec.p: The median follow‐up period for the cohort was 7.3 years (IQR 3.1–14.2). Second‐line (2L) therapy for PBC was initiated in 74 patients (15.8%), of whom 19 (15.3%) had MASLD, 7 (20.6%) cSLD, and 48 (15.4%) classic PBC, with no significant differences between groups (p = 0.726). The statistical handling of patients who received 2L in the context of follow‐up and event analysis has been detailed in the Methods section. During this time, 67 patients (14.3%) developed at least one liver‐related event, with the most frequent being the development of cirrhosis (n = 42, 62.7%), followed by ascites (n = 7, 10.4%) (Figure 3). The median time to the first liver‐related event, considering only those who presented any event‐ was 8.6 years (IQR 3.1–10.5). These events occurred in 20 patients with MASLD (16.1%), four patients with cSLD (11.8%), and 43 patients with classic PBC (13.8%), with no significant differences between groups (p = 0.9). Additionally, there were no differences in the type of liver‐related event across the groups (p = 0.822).

caption.p: Kaplan–Meier curves for liver‐related event‐free survival according to the presence or absence of steatotic liver disease (SLD) in patients with Primary Biliary Cholangitis (PBC).

sec.p: In the univariate analysis, male sex, lack of Paris II response at 1 year, an unfavourable GLOBE score at 1 year, baseline liver stiffness > 8 kPa, and IgM > 240 mg/dL at 1 year were all associated with an increased risk of developing liver‐related events during follow‐up. However, the presence of MASLD or cSLD, whether analysed separately or together, was not associated with a higher risk of liver‐related events during follow‐up.

To evaluate the risk of liver‐related events, we developed two multivariate models to avoid collinearity between variables related to response criteria. Only variables with sufficient available data were included in the modelling process, with those excluded summarized in Table S3. Despite the lack of association between MASLD and event risk in the univariate analysis, this variable was retained in the multivariate models due to its relevance to the study aims. Across the models, lack of treatment response at 1 year and male sex were consistently associated with an increased risk of liver‐related events. In contrast, MASLD was not associated with a higher risk of event development in any model (Table 3).

caption.p: Univariate and multivariate Cox regression analysis for liver‐related events.

tr.td: No response Paris II at 1 year

fn.p: Note: Bold value indicates statistically significant values with a p‐value below 0.05.
Abbreviations: cSLD, cryptogenic steatosis; MASLD, metabolic dysfunction‐associated steatotic liver disease.

sec.p: Since patients initiating 2L therapy were censored at the time of 2L initiation in the primary analysis, a sensitivity analysis was performed to assess the robustness of these findings. For this analysis, initiation of 2L was treated as a competing risk for the development of liver‐related events, using the Fine‐Gray subdistribution hazard model, retaining patients who initiate 2L therapy in the risk set rather than removing them through censoring, thereby accounting for the possibility that these patients may carry a different baseline risk profile. The results of the sensitivity analysis were consistent with those of the primary analysis: the presence of SLD at baseline was not associated with an increased risk of liver‐related events (sHR 1.18; 95% CI 0.70–2.00), and the variables previously identified as independent predictors in the primary analysis retained their prognostic significance (Table S4).

Furthermore, as shown in Table 1, patients with PBC‐SLD had significantly lower ALP levels. Therefore, we conducted a propensity score IPTW analysis, in which we again found no impact of steatosis on the development of hepatic events (HR 1.62; 95% CI 0.92–2.83). The balance before and after IPTW, measured by standardized differences (STD), is shown in Figure S2.

sec.title: Cardiovascular Events
sec.p: Data on cardiovascular events were available for 254 patients in the cohort (54.1%). Of these, 61 patients (24%) developed at least one event, occurring in 18 patients (24.6%) with PBC‐SLD and 43 patients (23.7%) with classic PBC, with no significant differences between groups (p = 0.879). The distribution of cardiovascular event types is shown in Table S5.

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: This study shows that, in a large real‐world cohort of patients, the concomitant presence of SLD and PBC at the moment of PBC diagnosis does not appear to impact on the progression of the liver disease. Accurate characterization of SLD is a key aspect of this project, as is the definition of MASLD. For this reason, the study was conducted in accordance with the most recent available consensus [14], in which the diagnosis of MASLD is defined by the presence of steatosis on any imaging modality along with at least one cardiometabolic risk factor, without the need of a histological sample. Therefore, in this study, the identification of steatosis was carried out strictly in accordance with the specifications of the most recent EASL guidelines [13] and the latest consensus statement [14]. In terms of CAP, individuals with a CAP‐value > 275 dB/m were considered to have steatosis. Although this threshold may be considered high for the diagnosis of steatosis, we deemed it essential to avoid overestimating its presence by selecting a cutoff with a sensitivity greater than 90% for steatosis identification [8]. In this regard, it is important to highlight that only patients with reliable information regarding the presence or absence of steatosis were included, with special attention to those without steatosis.

In our study, we observed that patients with steatosis exhibited significantly lower ALP levels compared to those with classic PBC, findings consistent with previous reports [19]. While this could initially suggest that patients with baseline PBC and steatosis may have less liver injury, given that ALP reflects cholangiocellular damage, and thus a potentially more favourable condition, the proportion of patients with a baseline GLOBE score > 0.5 [20] was similar in both groups (21.9% in patients without steatosis vs. 21% in those with steatosis; p = 0.849). Nevertheless, to address this potential limitation in interpretability, we calculated the percentage reduction in ALP during the first year. Although patients with classic PBC had higher baseline ALP levels, their relative reduction was significantly greater than that observed in PBC‐SLD patients. We then applied IPTW propensity score to balance the cohorts. As shown in Figure S2, the cohorts were generally well balanced before weighting, except for ALP distribution; this imbalance was corrected after IPTW. Following adjustment, SLD again showed no impact on the risk of hepatic events.

Another relevant finding of our study was that patients with steatosis exhibited significantly higher LSM compared to those without steatosis. Furthermore, the proportion of patients with LSM values exceeding 8 kPa [20] was also significantly higher among those with MASLD. Notably, no patient with cSLD had liver stiffness values above 8 kPa, as shown in Table 2. However, it is important to consider that this numerical difference at baseline may be related to the fact that, in patients with MASLD and obesity, liver stiffness measurements can overestimate the actual severity of fibrosis [21, 22] and that cholestasis itself may modify and act as a confounder when interpreting transient elastography results [23]. Actually, abstract results presented by Lytvyak et al. [24] showed that patients with steatosis had higher LSM values, although they narrowly missed statistical significance. Strikingly, in that study, the LSM reported were remarkably elevated in both groups: 13.7 kPa in PBC patients with steatosis and 11.4 kPa in those without. These values are considerably higher than those typically observed at the time of PBC diagnosis [20]. However, in our study, since the presence of SLD was assessed predominantly by ultrasound, it could be argued that the ability to detect steatosis might be limited in patients with more advanced fibrosis, thereby reducing the interpretability of the results. Nevertheless, the findings regarding response prediction and prognosis remain consistent when excluding patients with liver stiffness measurement exceeding 10 kPa and those with established cirrhosis (Table S6).

Having addressed these considerations, the main finding of our study is that the presence of SLD at baseline does not impact the clinical course of patients with PBC. We evaluated both the 1‐year treatment response and 1‐year prognosis using the most widely validated scoring systems. Furthermore, to achieve a more refined stratification, patients were divided into three groups: classical PBC, PBC with cSLD and PBC with MASLD.

Following this categorization, we found no differences in terms of UDCA response as measured by the Paris II criteria, nor using more stringent endpoints such as normalization of ALP, deep response or complete biochemical normalization. Similarly, no differences were observed in prognostic scores, including GLOBE and UK‐PBC.

Additionally, 14.3% of patients had hepatic events during follow‐up, similar to rates in classic PBC cohorts [25]. Importantly, the occurrence of these events was associated with well‐established prognostic factors in PBC [26], and not with the presence of SLD. In this regard, patients who failed to respond to treatment or who had an unfavourable GLOBE score at 1 year were those at highest risk of developing hepatic events during follow‐up. Notably, although complete biochemical normalization did not reach independent predictive significance in the model, the crude analysis showed a near‐significant difference in event rates between those achieving and those not achieving normalization (9.1% vs. 16.7%; p = 0.054). The clinical relevance of this finding should not be dismissed, as the relatively low number of events observed for this endpoint may have precluded a more definitive statistical conclusion.

Our findings are consistent with those reported by Minuk et al. [19], at least regarding the message that MASLD does not impact the course of PBC. However, unlike our study, their assessment of risk and prognosis was focused almost exclusively on the progression of FIB‐4 and APRI scores, without considering 1‐year treatment response or LSM. Similarly, Lytvyak et al. also concluded that SLD does not affect the progression of PBC. Interestingly, however, patients with SLD in their cohort demonstrated a greater treatment response at 1 year. Moreover, the proportion of classic PBC patients who achieved a Paris II response at 1 year was lower than expected [27]. In contrast, in our cohort, approximately 30%–40% of patients were non‐responders to UDCA, findings consistent with expectations for this population. Additionally, as mentioned in the previous paragraph, TE values in our study were also within expected ranges for the newly diagnosed PBC. Therefore, while the overarching message may be similar across studies, we believe our cohort accurately reflects the real‐world profile of patients living with this disease. Moreover, to ensure methodological rigour in the assessment of the risk of events under UDCA, patients who initiated 2L were censored at the time of treatment escalation. Given that this censoring may not be fully non‐informative—as patients escalating therapy may carry a different risk profile—a sensitivity analysis using the Fine‐Gray competing risks regression model was performed, consistently showing that the presence of SLD at diagnosis was not associated with the risk of liver‐related events during follow‐up.

In contrast to our findings, Hernández‐Pérez et al. [4] concluded that the presence of MASLD negatively affects the prognosis of PBC patients. However, some methodological limitations warrant cautious interpretation. First, the cohort included only 36 PBC‐MASLD patients, with a prevalence of MASH higher than expected (36.1%). Second, while the multivariable analysis used Paris II failure as a proxy for poor outcomes, hard clinical outcomes were only compared between groups without survival analysis accounting for time to event, not accounting for the duration of exposure or censoring, and follow up between groups was statistically different. Third, patients with PBC‐MASLD were significantly older than those without (57.4 vs. 51.7 years, p = 0.013), and since age is a direct component of the GLOBE score formula, this disparity alone would be expected to inflate GLOBE scores in the MASLD group irrespective of any true difference in disease severity. Consistent with our findings, Ergenc et al. [28]—in a letter of response to Hernández‐Pérez—reported contrasting results from their own cohort, in which PBC‐MASLD patients showed a significantly higher UDCA response rate than PBC‐only patients and they suggest that steatotic liver disease may not negatively impact prognosis in PBC [4].

It is well known that SLD can worsen the course of various liver diseases [29, 30]. However, its effect on autoimmune liver disease remains less clear [31, 32]. Whereas the diagnosis of PBC involves evidence of bile duct injury, steatosis does not necessarily imply hepatocellular death. Besides, the accumulation of bile acids in the liver because of cholestasis in PBC could result in FXR activation and prevention of lipid absorption, protecting against MASLD [33]. Furthermore, bile acid synthesis, bile composition and the enterohepatic circulation are also altered in MASH patients [34], which could enhance the pathophysiological mechanisms of PBC. However, UDCA treatment might preclude the activation of those pathways by reducing hepatic steatosis [35], apoptosis [36] and inflammation [37] and by regulating mitochondrial function and energy homeostasis [38] and modulating bile composition and insulin sensitivity [39]. In fact, although UDCA therapy has no significant effects on liver histology in patients with steatohepatitis, it can effectively promote cytoprotection [40] and reduce serum levels of ALT, GGT and cardiovascular risk [41]. Thus, UDCA pharmacological effect on steatosis in PBC patients cannot be ruled out.

We acknowledge that this study has certain limitations. First, it is a retrospective multicentre study involving 35 centres, which, while constituting the largest published cohort of patients with available data on SLD in PBC with long‐term follow‐up, implies inherent heterogeneity in clinical practice and in the methods used to assess steatosis across participating centres. Second, information regarding the presence of SLD was only available at the time of diagnosis, and the evolution of SLD over time may modify the risk profile of these patients; prospective studies with longitudinal assessment of steatosis will be needed to address this question. Third, steatosis was primarily diagnosed through imaging studies, thus, not all included patients had a CAP measurement, preventing stratification based on steatosis severity. Nevertheless, the diagnosis of steatosis was conducted according to current recommendations [14], which specify that the identification of steatosis by imaging modalities is sufficient for the diagnosis of SLD [13, 14]. Accordingly, only patients with reliable information confirming the presence or absence of steatosis were included. Fourth, information on waist circumference was only available for approximately one‐quarter of patients, which may have led to underdiagnosis of metabolic syndrome in some cases. However, since central obesity rarely constitutes an isolated criterion for metabolic syndrome and the remaining diagnostic components were collected, we consider the impact of this limitation on MASLD phenotyping to be modest. Fifth, patients who initiated second‐line therapy were censored at the time of treatment escalation in the primary survival analysis. While this on‐treatment approach is methodologically appropriate given the study objective, censoring at this time point may not be fully non‐informative, as patients escalating therapy may carry a different risk profile; this potential limitation was addressed through a sensitivity analysis using the Fine‐Gray competing risks model, which yielded consistent results. While these limitations may have introduced some degree of conservative bias, they are unlikely to have substantially altered the overall interpretation of our findings.
In conclusion, our study shows that the presence of hepatic SLD at baseline in patients with PBC appears to be common, but does not seem to worsen the prognosis of the disease.

caption.p: Table S1: Baseline characteristics comparing patients with MASLD to those without steatosis (Classic PBC).
Table S2: UDCA response criteria at 12 months according to SLD status.
Table S3: Univariate analysis of the risk of developing a liver‐related event and available information. OR, odds ratio.
Table S4: Sensitivity analysis for liver‐related events using the Fine‐Gray competing risks regression model, considering initiation of second‐line therapy as a competing event.
Table S5: Cardiovascular events per group.
Table S6: Univariate analysis of the association between SLD and (a) biochemical response to UDCA and (b) liver‐related event risk in patients without baseline cirrhosis and LSM < 10 kPa.
Figure S1: Flowchart of patients included in the study.

sec.title: Data Availability Statement
sec.p: Data are available on request due to privacy/ethical restrictions.
